videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Chiang Mai, Thailand, and in this video we're going to show you the best things to do in and around the city. So hope you enjoy it. Chiang Mai is located in the northwest of Thailand. It's one of the largest and most popular cities to visit in the country for good reasons. Founded in the 13th century, Chiang Mai has a rich history and is home to stunning temples and architecture. But what makes the city truly special is its unique culture, friendly locals, delicious cuisine, and relaxed atmosphere. In this video, we highlight the best things to do in Chiang Mai as well as in its beautiful surrounding mountains. Now let's dive in. We're Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Some of Thailand's most delicious food can be found in the north, and in Chiang Mai specifically the staple dish is Khao Soi. This creamy yellow curry noodle soup comes with a choice of chicken or beef and you can find it in nearly any restaurant in the city. But be aware it's usually served as lunch, so many of the popular Khao Soi restaurants close around 2 or 3 P.M. We'll also highlight other tasty dishes to try in Chiang Mai later in this video. There are over 300 Buddhist temples in Chiang Mai and many of them are located inside the historic old town. The most iconic and beautiful ones are located within walking distance from each other. These include the impressive Wat Phra Singh, Wat Chiang Man, Wat Phan Tao, and the ruins of Wat Chedi Luang. Most of the temples can be visited for free though donations are always appreciated. Visiting an elephant sanctuary in Chiang Mai is on most travelers' bucket lists, but finding one that's ethical can be tough. Unfortunately, Thailand has a history of animal abuse and captive elephants are sometimes forced to swim with tourists and carry them on their back, which can be harmful for these gentle giants. In Chiang Mai, there are many sanctuaries that claim to be ethical but in practice that's not always true. We visited Elephant Nature Park, a sanctuary that's committed to education and rehabilitation of elephants that have been abused by the tourism industry. We observed the roaming elephants for a few hours and helped with feeding. It was a relaxing and rewarding experience. And we also had a chance to talk to the founder of Save Elephant Foundation. Besides indulging in the delicious Thai food, it's also fun to learn how to cook the dishes. Chiang Mai is the most popular place in Thailand for cooking classes, with a wide variety to choose from. You'll usually start your class by going to the market and learning about the Thai produce before making a few dishes at your individual cooking station and finally eating your creations. We joined a half-day cooking class during which we prepared three different dishes: a Thai soup, a curry, and a Pad Thai. For each dish, there are different options to choose from for your proteins and flavors. And as a bonus, we also got mango sticky rice for dessert. We left a link to the class that we joined in the description below. The Mae Ping River is the main river that runs through Chiang Mai and offers a quick escape from the city. You can rent a kayak and cruise down yourself or join a two-hour boat tour that takes you past beautiful scenery around Chiang Mai. Along the river, you can see traditional teak houses and a lot of rural greenery. The boat tour also makes a stop at a local farm, where you learn about different plants and trees in the area as well as enjoy some tea and food. While the city of Chiang Mai itself is fascinating, the surrounding mountain scenery is spectacular. Home to Thailand's highest mountain, Doi Inthanon National Park is about a one and a half hour drive from the city. The park is filled with beautiful waterfalls, hiking trails, and viewpoints. And there are also twin Pagodas near the summit of Doi Inthanon, which are surrounded by a gorgeous flower garden. You can easily spend a full day here and we added a link to a day tour from Chiang Mai in the description. So right after visiting the viewpoint for the sunrise you can come to these Pagodas, because it's really really quiet and normally it gets quite crowded. But just make sure that you bring some warm clothes because it does get really cold. For an authentic Thai market experience, head to Talat Warorot in the old town. This historic market opened in the early 20th century and is a great place to buy clothes, souvenirs, local food and spices. Opening its doors daily from 6 AM to 7 PM, this is the market where lots of locals shop. The most famous attraction in Chiang Mai is undoubtedly the sacred Doi Suthep Temple, about 15 kilometers or 9 miles outside the city center. The temple is a beautiful example of Northern Thai architecture and the Pagoda supposedly contains relics of Buddha himself. If you visit around 6 PM, you can also listen to the monks chant. And as a bonus, the temple offers beautiful views of Chiang Mai and the surrounding area. It's a great place to watch sunset from. Another fun day activity in Chiang Mai is to rent a scooter and drive the Samoeng Loop. This 100 kilometer or 62 mile route is a pretty easy ride with spectacular views winding through the Mae Sa Valley and the Samoeng Forest. It takes about four to five hours to complete the loop depending on the amount of stops that you make along the way. We definitely recommend visiting the Mae Sa waterfalls as well as one of the botanical gardens, a coffee shop with a view, and the Samoeng viewpoint. But of course there are a lot of other things to do and see along the way. Just make sure you have a little bit of scooter driving experience before going, especially for the parts when navigating through the city. For a more challenging multi-day route, we highly recommend driving the Mae Hong Son Loop, and we have a full video about that on our channel, so check it out if you're interested! The largest and most important market in Chiang Mai is the Sunday Night Market in the heart of the old town. Starting from the Tha Pae Gate, the market runs along the entire length of the Ratchadamnoen Road. From 5 PM until around 10:30PM, hundreds of stalls sell handmade goods, souvenirs, and a diverse range of food for both tourists and locals alike. The market is vibrant, colorful, and social, providing a great opportunity to sample the unique cuisine. But if you're not around on Sunday, it's also possible to visit the Saturday Night Market or the Chang Puak Gate Market on any other day of the week. So if you're looking for a souvenir in Thailand, this might be one of the best markets to do that. We've been to a lot of them but this one's just really well put together and it has really high-quality stuff. There are a few other things to do in Chiang Mai that we didn't mention and we've included a list on the screen here. If you're planning your Thailand trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code below. We also included important links that you'll need for your Chiang Mai trip in the description, such as a blog post with places to stay, eat, and drink, as well as an interactive map so you can get to each place that we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure.

In this video I share everything you will need to have the ultimate 3 days in Chiang Mai. In this itinerary you'll find out the best day trips to take from the city, the best temples to visit, how to experience the regions famous elephants and cuisine, as well as the secret bets nightlife spots
videos.transcript: Most people waste their time in Chiang Mai just wandering around in circles. But in just three days, you can experience its most incredible sites, temples, and nightife like a pro. I visited Chiang Mai many times over the last 10 years. And this itinerary will show you how to make every moment count, including how to actually explore the old town without getting tired of all the temples, the most stunning nature spots just a short ride from the center, and where to find Chiang Mai's secret nightlife spots. Chi Mai's old town is a mustsea, but most people make one big mistake. They just wander around aimlessly hoping to stumble on something interesting. But if you know where to go, this place is a gold mine of history, culture, and hidden gems. First, let's start the day right with coffee. And not just any coffee, Chiang Mai is home to some of the best in the world. Thanks to the perfect mix of altitude, soil, and rainfall, Northern Thailand produces some incredible tasting coffee beans. If you're near Tapé Gate, you'll find some of the trendiest cafes in the city. Whether you want a strong espresso, a slow drip brew, or a creamy Thai iced coffee, this is the perfect way to start your day. Stop two. Changai's old town has over 30 temples. That's way too many to see in one day. So, let's focus on the three that truly stand out. But which are the best temples within the old town? In the heart of Chiang Mai's old town stands the ruins of what was once the tallest structure in the city. Towering over everything and impressive 82 m. Built centuries ago, this ancient temple was a symbol of power for the Lana Kingdom. But fate had other plans. A devastating earthquake struck, reducing its grand spire to rubble and forever altering the skyline of Chmai. Despite its damage, the temple remains one of the most awinspiring sites in the city. This is what Ched Lang once the pride of the Lana Kingdom and still one of Mangai's most breathtaking landmarks. But the next temple is known for its stunning golden stuper and its classic Lana style architecture. If you come early in the morning, you might even catch monks receiving arms, a peaceful and a spiritual experience. This golden landmark is called Wra Singh and it's just a 10-minute walk from Wed. But hidden in a quiet corner of Oldtown is Changai's oldest temple, dating back to 1296. Beyond its ancient cheddies and ly halls, it offers something rare, a peaceful garden, perfect for escaping the city's buzz. This is Wat Chang man, and it's the old town's most peaceful historic spot. Stop three. As you explore, don't miss the ancient walls and moes surrounding the old town. They were originally built to protect the city and now make for some of the best photo spots in Chiang Mai. Tap Gate is the most famous part of the wall and depending on when you visit, you might catch street performers, live music, or even a cultural festival happening right here. Stop four, the most unique massages in Thailand. After all that walking, you're going to need to rest your feet. And Chang Mai is home to some of the best and weirdest massages in the world. If you're on a budget, you can get a foot massage right on the street for about 200 Bart, which is probably the cheapest out of anywhere I've ever seen in Thailand. But for a more unique experience, check out the X-Convict massage parlor. Now, that might sound a bit strange, but most of the women here weren't hardened criminals. They were farmers. Northern Thailand is part of the Golden Triangle, which was once a major hub for opium production. Many locals were involved in the growing opium trade simply as a way to make a living. Today, they've been rehabilitated and retrained as skilled massage therapists, turning their lives around, offering some of the best massages in the city. But there's another unique massage option. Blind massage therapists. Some people say they actually give the best massages because of their heightened sense of touch, which helps them to find areas of stress in the body more effectively. And there are a number of massage parlor that focus exclusively on blind massages. But you can't leave the old town without trying the ultimate Chiang Mai dish. This is a rich coconut-based curry with egg noodles, usually served with chicken, and topped with crispy fried noodles for extra texture. It's known as cow soy. It's creamy, spicy, and absolutely delicious. And you'll find it served all throughout the old town. Now, there are a number of places that claim to have the best cow soy in Oldtown. And sometimes you'll see cues all the way down the street, but personally, I've never known these to be much better than the sanded cow soy, which is absolutely delicious no matter where I've ordered it. But I have one more bonus stop for you. If you're in town on a Sunday, you're in for a treat, because that's when one of Thailand's best night markets takes over the old town. Starting around 400 p.m., the streets fill up with vendors selling everything from handmade crafts to some of the best street food in the country. Unlike some markets that focus only on souvenirs, this one has a little bit of everything. Whether you're looking for a unique gift or just want to snack your way through Chiang Mai, this is the place to be. But as nightfalls, it's time to experience a different side of Chiang Mai. While the city is known for its laid-back, no frrills nightife, there's a hidden upscale scene that most visitors miss. For a truly unique cocktail experience, head to Tub Bar, an intimate 12 seat spot where every drink is inspired by local villages surrounding Chiang Mai. Expect flavors you wouldn't find anywhere else in the world. Think coconut rum, garlic based syrup, and even Thai mushrooms. With limited seating, it's best to get there early or make a reservation. But if fine dining is more your style, leave the old town and head towards the Miping River where Chiang Mai's best restaurants line the waterfront. For the ultimate experience, time your visit around sunset. The views over the river are some of the best in the city. But there is much more to Chiang Mai than just the old town. In fact, the real beauty of Chiang Mai lies beyond the city. Vast mountains, hidden waterfalls, and remote villages are waiting to be explored. On day two, I'll show you the three best options for day trips from around Chiang Mai. About an hour's drive from Chiang Mai, deep in the Misty Mountains, lies a village famous for its stunning scenery and worldclass coffee. Nestled among lush forests, this small community is home to the Karen ethnic group who have lived here for generations. What makes this place so special? Nearly every house is built from a traditional wood blending seamlessly into the landscape. The air is filled with the aroma of freshly roasted coffee. And as you climb the steep wounding mountain path, you'll pass street food vendors selling local snacks. The best way to take it all in, grab a coffee at a mountaintop cafe with panoramic views of the valley below, or sip your drink beside a peaceful waterfall surrounded by nothing but the sound of flowing water. This is the town of Meampong, one of northern Thailand's most charming and peaceful villages. But if you're up for a more active adventure, Thailand's highest peak is just a 2-hour drive away from Chiang Mai. This day trip takes you through dense jungle, hidden waterfalls, and rice patties offering some of the most scenic trekking in the country. At the summit, you'll find the twin pagodas built to honor the former king and queen of Thailand. On a clear day, the views stretch for miles over the misty mountains. But the real highlight is the trek itself. You'll hike through the cool cloud covered rainforest, home to rare plants and wildlife. And to end the trip, you'll visit a hill tribe where you can learn about the unique culture and traditions of the local people. This is Doyenthanon, Chiang Mai's most famous trekking destination. But for the most varied and unforgettable day trip, you need to head north toward the town of Changda, a region known for its towering mountains, lush jungles, and hidden gems that most tourists never see. Start your day with a trip to Araxa Tea Garden, a picturesque plantation where you can walk through rolling green hills, learn how tea leaves are harvested, and even try your hand at picking them yourself. From there, you can make your way to W Banden, one of the most underrated temples in the whole of Thailand. Tucked away in the mountains, this hidden gem features intricately designed statues, striking blue rooftops, and a peaceful atmosphere that sets it apart from the more tourist heavy temples. For a midday refresh, stop at the sticky waterfalls or Btown waterfalls. A natural wonder where the limestone covered rocks create a grip strong enough to let you walk up the waterfall without slipping. It's one of the most unique experiences in northern Thailand, and it's the perfect way to cool off. Before heading back, unwind at a natural hotring. And finally, end your day in Changda, a peaceful town at the base of Thailand's third highest mountain. As the sun sets behind the dramatic cliffs, you'll see why this spot is known for one of the most stunning golden hour views in Thailand. Miss this trip and you'll miss some of Northern Thailand's most incredible experiences. But after a full day of exploring, it's time to dive back into the city's vibrant night life. If you miss the Sunday night market, check out the night bazaar, where vendors sell everything from handcrafted souvenirs to some of the best street food in Chiang Mai. running every night. It's the perfect place to grab affordable gifts and sample local flavors and experience the city's lively evening atmosphere. If you want a truly unique way to end the night, though, head to Changai's Muai Stadium for an authentic Thai experience. There are a number of stadiums in the city, but this one is located conveniently by the night bazaar to the east of the old town. But on day three, you need to really get to grips with the aspects that make northern Thailand so unique. No trip to Chiang Mai is complete without spending time with Thailand's national animal, the elephant. But there are sanctuaries all over the country. So why do you need to do it in Chiang Mai? Because northern Thailand is where elephants naturally belong. Unlike the southern islands or areas like Patia, this region has vast forests, rivers, and mountains, giving elephants the space they need to live as naturally as possible. Chiang Mai is also home to some of the most respected and ethical elephant sanctuaries in the country. Places that focus on rescue, rehabilitation, and conservation rather than the tourist entertainment. When you visit Chiang Mai Elephant Sanctuary, you're not just seeing elephants. You're actively supporting responsible tourism. Instead of riding them or watching them perform tricks, you'll get to feed them, walk them, or bathe with them in a way that respects their well-being. If you want to experience elephants the right way, this is the place to do it. But if you really want to understand northern Thai culture, you need to understand its cuisine. Unlike Bangkok or the islands, where food is more influenced by international flavors, northern Thai cuisine has stayed true to its roots with deep influences from Myamar, Lao, and China. And that's why there's no better place in the country to take a Thai cooking class. Chiang Mai is surrounded by far and fresh markets, meaning almost every ingredient comes straight from the local area. Many cooking schools here even have their own organic gardens where you can pick fresh herbs and vegetables before you cook. Another reason Chiang Mai stands out, the variety of dishes. While pad thai and green curry dominate menus in the south, Northern Thailand has its own unique specialtities that you won't find anywhere else. I've already mentioned how cow soy is one of the best dishes in the country, but there's also sio herbal northern sausages or namp spicy tomato chili dip. Taking a Thai cooking class in Chiang Mai is an unforgettable experience. But even with the perfect itinerary, there are plenty of mistakes that can ruin your trip to Chiang Mai. Watch this video here to learn the 16 things I wish I knew before visiting Chiang Mai, so you can avoid making all the same mistakes that I did.

an overnight Trek Through the Jungle there are lots of different tracks you can do depending on your level of fitness and Hiking experience some of them are quite short but there are over multiple day tracks for more experienced hikers you'll spend the night staying in a local Village sometimes in the middle of the Jungle the TR will be led by someone from the area so not only do you not have to worry about getting lost but it's also the best way to find out how the people of Northern Thailand really live as well as to find out how they've maintained their cultures throughout the years if you're worried about the wildlife the areas around aren't quite as wild as of national parks so you don't need to be quite so worried about snakes and spiders but you might actually have the chance to see elephants out in the wild rather than a sanctuary another cool way to explore the area is by scooter or motorbike the samong loop is a 1day bike trip that starts and ends in chai but one of the most famous bike routs in Thailand is a 4-day trip that takes you to the towns of Mayon song and Pie and it winds through the mountains of Northern Thailand this route is called the May Hong song loot but be careful though because it's also the most deadly place in the country to ride for foreigners last year around 30 tourists died riding motor bags around chamai chamai is the city that's most famous for its stunning temples but some of them are a complete waste of time here are the temples actually worth visiting probably the easiest place to explore the temples is the Old Town the old town was founded in 1296 as the capital of the Lana Kingdom and has kept its historical charm with ancient city walls and Moes still visible today there are more than 30 temples still standing within the Old Town including wat ched lwang which is one of the biggest and oldest this Temple was partially destroyed by an earthquake in the 1500s but it's still standing today but what changai does better than anywhere else in Thailand is Mountain temples to reach these you have to travel a little further outside of the city but it's well worth the effort there's one Temple that sits overlooking the city its most famous monument is the golden ched that sits at the center of the temple it's called doep and it could easily be reached by taxi within about 20 minutes of the Old Town there are two stunning pagodas dedicated to the former king and queen of Thailand that lie over 2,500 m above sea level and they're called the twin pagodas at Doon National Park however there's one temple on the outskirts of the city that I believe to be the most underrated temple in all of Thailand despite only being an hour's drive away from chamai this Temple is rarely visited by Foreign tourists probably because it's still not really in the guide books or travel Vlogs that I've seen in fact most people travel hours from changai to see the temples of Chang raai but I think this Temple is just as stunning and much closer the temple has a stunning Mountain background magnificent buddist sculptures and a beautiful mix of traditional and modern Lana architecture the temple is called W banden and it's my favorite temple in chai chamai is the best place in the country to experience Thailand's National Festival every year people flock to the city to experience Thailand's unique Traditions one of the best times to visit is during sran this is held in mid April every year and involves a massive 3-day water fight but if you're looking for something more Serene you should visit during ly cron or yeen in November at Ying the chanai night sky will light up with beautiful lanterns and ly quong involves gathering around lakes rivers and canals to pay respects to the goddess of Water by releasing beautiful Lotus sha wrath decorated with candles incense some flowers into the w visiting during these times will be an unforgettable experience but there is one time you absolutely should not visit chamai there are almost 3 months of the year when visiting the city is dangerous and can leave some longlasting negative impacts on your health so why is it so bad chamai is still agricultural so in order to fertilize the fields around the city they burn the crops at the end of every year because it helps the so to grow better crops for the next Harvest this Burning takes over the entire city and during February and March the city becomes one of the most polluted cities in the whole world if you have any respiratory diseases it can be quite dangerous but even if you don't this is not going to be a fun time to visit chay's home is some fascinating local markets but finding where and when they run can actually be quite confusing in fact there's been multiple times when I've turned up expecting that to be a market and it just wasn't there one of China's most famous markets runs every evening from about 5:00 p.m. it sells a lot of clothes and handycrafts as well as some street food it's on chlan road which is where a lot of bars and restaurants are and it's simply called the night Bazar are but some of the best night markets in the city run only once a week the two best are the Saturday night market that lies on WOAI Road at the south of the Oldtown and the Sunday market that runs through the center of the old town from tape gate but there's a darker side of chamai Tourism one that not only aims to rip you off but also exploits the local people some of the most vulnerable communities in the areas are the tribal communities now I've already mentioned visiting some of these troops as part of a track or a day trip but it's important to do a little research on these tours beforehand to make sure where the money is going and how it's being used one of the most controversial issues among the Hill tribe communities are the long neck Karin Community the Karen are a community that have lived in the area for centuries and one of their Traditions is elongating the necks of their women by using golden rings that stretch their necks the stretching of the neck can lead to health problems in later life as the muscles around the neck become weak for the practice to work you have to start it from a young age and in some cases the women have been forced into tradition so that they can make money for their communities as a tourist attraction now I'm not an expert on the Traditions within the communities so I should stress that I don't know to what extent they forc into it but what I do know is that some of the communities that you can visit are at best tourist traps and at worst exploitive I visited one of these in the past and it was one of the saddest experiences that I've had in Thailand as soon as re ended the women of the tribe started asking for money for food they had a market store with souvenirs so we agreed to buy quite a few items from them but this place kind of felt like a human Zoo where the people weren't very well looked after taxi drivers in changai might offer to take you to some of these Villages it's possible that they're getting a kickback from taking you there but it's definitely an experience that you should avoid but there is one more trap that you need to avoid in chai and if you're not careful of it you can end up supporting animal cruelty elephant riding is a tradition in Thailand where a seat is placed on the back of an elephant to allow the person to ride the animal this is a problem because to get the elephants to do this it requires harsh training methods that involves beating the animals and over time the act of riding the elephant damages the elephant's spine causing it a lot of pain what's worse is that some of the elephants will be kept in Crown dirty conditions so it seems simple right just don't ride elephants and visit the places that elephant rid it well unfortunately things aren't quite that simple unethical sanctuaries use different tricks to hide their analog use and some will outright lie to the customers I remember when I first visited chamai I did a guarded track that included bathing and feeding elephants and it stressed that it was a no riding experience now as soon as the elephants arrived we realized something was a bit off as they were quite aggressive with the elephants but as soon as we were done bathing them the keepers took the elephants across the river where the next group of tourists immediately got on their backs and began riding them one common trick that these companies use is frequently changing the name of their company so that they can avoid bad reviews online or even using similar names to sanctuaries that are more reputable but my biggest regret from traveling in chamai is staying in crappy hotels what I didn't realize is that chamai is actually home that's some of the best and cheapest luxury resorts in the country watch this video here to find out how I stayed in some of the best results in Thailand without breaking the bank

videos.transcript: We absolutely love Mai and have visited this city many times. In this video, we'll show you 10 things to do. And at the end, we have a bonus tip, which is also our favorite thing to do in Chiang Mai. So, yes, this is actually a top 11, but that sounded weird, so we're sticking with top 10 plus bonus. For all activities, we'll leave a link in the description for more information. Chiang Mai is located in the north of Thailand and is rich with history and culture. But as it's the second city of Thailand, there are also many conveniences such as an airport, shopping malls, and of course, lots of food. All right, let's get into it. Our first recommendation is an easy one, but something that has to be done when you're in Mai. Walking through the old town. Yes, walking. The old town of Chiang Mai is a square surrounded by a moat making it super easy to navigate. Inside the old city, you will find surprises around every corner. There are so many hidden streets with artwork, small cafes with some of the best coffee, temples everywhere, and lots of food. You can also visit the different gates of the old city wall. And the Taipei gate is the most famous one. It's the old main entrance into the city. And this is also a great starting point to start your walking tour. Next up, the Sunday Night Market. The most fun and famous market of Chiang Mai is the Sunday Night Market. As you guessed it, it's only on Sundays and it starts around 400 p.m. and goes on until late in the evening. It covers the big street from the Taipei Gate all the way up to the Watras Singh Temple. The street will be filled with all kinds of vendors that come from all over the region, and it's a good opportunity to shop for souvenirs. While visiting the market, you will also come across many beautiful temples. And it so happens that around the temples, you will find the food carts with all your Thai classics and not so classic foods. [Music] Also, if your feet get tired, there is an abundance of massage options everywhere. If one night market isn't enough, we recommend being in Chiangg Mai on a Saturday, too. So, you can visit the Saturday Night Market on the Wai Walking Street. This market is also from 400 p.m. until 11ish and takes place on Saturdays. It has many similar things and you can even get a handdrawn cartoon picture of yourself. >> Chiang Mai is famous for its delicious food and how fun is it to make it yourself? There is an abundance of cooking schools in the city and they all follow a similar plan. The first part of the class starts at a local market so you can learn about the ingredients that you're about to cook with. You'll then be taken to the cooking class and you can choose a variety of Thai dishes to prepare. Think of the famous cow soy, mango, sticky rice, papaya salad, and lots of curries. >> Is that all you got, Laura? >> Yes, that's all I got. >> A big difference though is that some cooking classes take part in the city itself and others take place on a farm outside of town. A cooking class is a really fun deep dive in Thai food culture, but fair warning, it can get very hot. Our favorite Thai food is causoy. And Chiang Mai happens to be the capital of cow soy. What is causoy, you ask? Well, causoy is a spicy, rich, and creamy coconut curry soup. It usually includes a tender chicken leg, egg noodles, and it's topped with crispy fried noodles. Mai has three main seasons, and perhaps more than any other city in Thailand, choosing the right time of the year is essential. The cool season runs from November through February. It offers the best weather with pleasant temperatures, clearer skies, and more comfortable days for walking and outdoor activities. But it's also when the city is busiest and most expensive. Things begin to shift in February when the agricultural burning season starts, which usually lasts through April. Air quality can deteriorate rapidly with extremely high PM2.5 levels, and this is typically the worst time of year to visit, especially for people with respiratory or cardiovascular issues. Conversely, the rainy season runs from May through October, and the early months from May through August are often underrated. Crowds thin out and prices drop while the air clears. And this period is typically defined by short, predictable daily downpours. But the months of September and October tend to bring heavier and more persistent rain. You'll find the lowest prices of the year, but conditions are not ideal. There are also two major holidays to be aware of. Sran, which takes place in midappril, is the Thai New Year festival marked by a nationwide water fight. And Chiang Mai is one of the best places to experience it. While Yeang and Ly Katong are famous for lantern releases and cultural events and take place in November, both events are fantastic, but crowds are dense and prices often hit their highest point of the year. There's no universally wrong time to visit Chiang Mai, but choosing the right season and knowing what you're walking into makes a huge difference. Some Chiang Mai trips feel smooth from day one, while others feel oddly inefficient, and in most cases, the reason traces back to choosing the right or wrong location and accommodations. Chiang Mai looks compact on a map, but walking distances are deceptive, and typically you have three options. Staying inside the old city moat puts you close to temples, cafes, and walkable streets. It's atmospheric and convenient for sightseeing, but the area has a very limited night life. In contrast, Niman is a modern cafeheavy area that attracts digital nomads and younger travelers. It has better sidewalks, newer buildings, and a high concentration of restaurants and bars, but it's a little bit further from the major attractions. Itinerary mistakes are also very common and it's normally the difference between people who love Chiang Mai and those who don't quite get it. The first mistake is assuming you should try to see everything. Chiang Mai has a high density of temples, viewpoints, cafes, nature spots, and nearby excursions. And when you see them listed online, they all sound individually reasonable. But if they're stacked together, they create days that feel rushed, disjointed, and oddly unsatisfying, even though you technically did a lot. Temples are the clearest example. There are dozens within the city alone and they're generally all beautiful and historically significant. But seeing 10 temples doesn't make the experience 10 times better. Instead, it leads to temple fatigue and an odd blurring of the entire day. A better approach is to choose a few representative attractions and visit them at a slower pace. Wattpra Singh and Watt Cheddy Luwang inside the old city give you scale and history while Wattpra tat doi sutep outside the city adds elevation views and broader context if you have time for an excursion and the same logic applies to side trips. Chiang Mai functions best as a hub surrounded by adventure activities, mountains, national parks, waterfalls and elephant sanctuaries. But some visitors try to fit in too many side activities, which quickly turns the trip into constant transit, especially since distances can be deceptive. Make sure to leave enough room to take in the experience and recover. Conversely, others fail to plan at all and inevitably find the city boring by day two or three. The key is to plan enough, but not too much. And your planning should be deliberate, especially when it comes to tour packages. There are many fantastic operators throughout the city, but you'll also find plenty of subpar tours with rushed activities, early endings, excessive time at stop off points, or misleading descriptions. Some are sold as packed full day excursions that start early and feel productive at first. But operators will add time consuming activities like ATV rides or ziplining. They receive a commission for bringing you. And by the time those activities are finished, you're told there's no longer enough time to visit the places you actually wanted to see. Transportation in Shiang Mai can be simple or confusing, and mostly it comes down to understanding your options and how they work. For most visitors, ride hailing apps are the most straightforward option. Grabb, bolt, and in drive work reliably across the city and well beyond the moat. Rides are fast and affordable. Pricing is transparent, and destinations are clear, helping you avoid most of the friction that comes from miscommunication, price negotiation, or route disputes. But if you want a little more adventure at a lower price, Song tow are converted pickup trucks that act as shared taxis. They're divided into six colors which indicate specific routes, but red song towels are the most common as they operate within the city. To hail one, simply stand at the roadside and raise your arm as it approaches. If it stops, tell the driver where you're going, and if he's heading in that direction, he'll nod and usually state the fair. Within the city, rides are typically inexpensive, but foreigners may be quoted higher prices. They're also not the quickest option as drivers may stop to pick up additional passengers, take detours, or decline trips altogether. For longer journeys, yellow song towels can be useful as they operate fixed routes to many major tourist attractions and depart from the bus terminal. While orange, blue, white, and green trucks serve less common fixed routes. Just remember to follow one simple rule. Always make sure the fair is clear before starting the ride to avoid any issues. There are also modern and aironditioned RTC city buses running fixed routes throughout the city. Simply wait at a stop, hop on, and pay the fair, which typically ranges from 30 to 50 bot, and you can track the journey using the Via Bus app. Finally, there are traditional taxis and tuk tuk. If you take a taxi, confirm the driver will go to your destination and ensure the meter is turned on or agree on a flat fair before starting the journey. For tuk tucks, you'll always need to negotiate the price, and they're usually the most expensive transportation option, providing a novelty experience. Always make sure to clearly establish the price before you begin the ride, and ensure that it's for the trip, as some drivers may claim that it's per person once you arrive. And with both taxis and tuk tuk, be wary of potential scams. Many drivers receive commissions for bringing customers to subpar businesses. and short change scams are not uncommon. Some visitors may also consider renting a car or motorbike. Major international car rental brands operate at the airport, and the process is straightforward and typically aligned with Western countries. You need a proper license with an international driver's permit to drive legally in Thailand. Conversely, motorbike shops will often rent bikes without checking your license, which technically must have the IDP with a motorcycle specification. And if you're involved in an accident without a proper license, insurance will typically refuse coverage. Scams can also occur. Some shops will conceal existing damage and later blame the renter, demanding exorbitant repair fees, while others have used spare keys or tracking devices to steal the bike, leaving the renter responsible for the loss. These cases are relatively rare, but they do happen, and it's best to check Google reviews closely before you rent to check for any red flags. And never leave your passport as a deposit. A photocopy and cash should always be sufficient. Mmai has a very limited night life and it's one of the aspects of the city that visitors can find disappointing. Understanding how it works will help you set realistic expectations. Chiang Mai may be Thailand's second largest city, but when it comes to nightife, you probably wouldn't realize it. Much of the city shuts down early, often around midnight. And even on weekends, it can feel very quiet, especially if you aren't sure where to go. And nightlife zones are fragmented with long stretches of quiet, dead space in between. Given the early closures, it's best to avoid wasting time walking between different areas. Instead, plan ahead and choose your area wisely to match your preferences. Ly Crow Road is a bar-heavy strip that runs from the old city toward the night bazaar. It's technically the red light district, but it's a far cry from Paya or Bangkok and is generally fairly relaxed, but it won't appeal to many visitors. Zoey in yellow is a popular backpacker hangout with a cluster of bars inside the old city. It stays open late and the energy typically doesn't pick up until past 10. If you head west, Nimon is the trendier modern area with nightclubs and cocktail bars, but most venues follow an Asianstyle setup with plenty of tables and relatively small dance floors. Groups usually arrive together in reserve tables, which can surprise visitors from Western countries who expect open dance floors and spontaneous social energy. And timing also matters for most venues. Arrive too early and it may be empty, but show up too late and it may already be winding down. And this can lead to a common nightlife scam. You might find yourself at a slow venue or leaving a bar as it closes and be approached by someone unusually friendly who suggests a cool place or a local bar. You arrive, order drinks, and when the bill comes, it's dramatically higher than expected, and bouncers may block the exit and demand payment. This scam isn't unique to Chiang Mai. It exists in cities around the world, but given the city's subdued nightife, it can catch visitors offguard. Be cautious about following recommendations and check the Google reviews before you enter. And as with taxis or tuk tucks, carry small bills to avoid any short change scams in nightlife venues. While it's best to avoid running tabs to preempt any bill padding, these aren't particularly common issues, but an ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure. The same is true for a few other potential hustles that may occur simply walking around in popular tourist areas, which can be broadly described as the friendly stranger scam. Someone starts a conversation and eventually tries to pull you into a private tour, sell miracle cures, or ask to see your money. They may be local or foreign. And the solution is simple. Ignore and walk away. And before you fly, make sure to check out my arrival guide for a full breakdown of what to expect in your first hour in the city. That's it for our guide to Chiang Mai. Let us know if you have any thoughts in the comments section. Have a safe and enjoyable

videos.description:
Poor air quality makes Chiang Mai, Thailand, the worst place to live as an expat — if you VALUE your HEALTH.  #chiangmai #thailand #expatlife 

Chiang Mai turns into a gas chamber every year. From mid-February to mid-April, toxic smoke engulfs the city, pushing AQI levels beyond 500+. March is the worst, with hospitals overflowing, flights delayed due to zero visibility, and even the healthiest residents struggling to breathe. 
The air is so polluted that spending a day outside can be like smoking multiple packs of cigarettes.

For 3 months, it becomes unlivable. That’s why, despite all its charm, Chiang Mai earns a  2/10 as a place to live long-term as an expat. 
Unless you plan to escape every March and April, you’ll be stuck choking on toxic air, trapped in your apartment with air purifiers running full blast.
